schema_notes: v0.2 probe shape (Sprint 3): paired content/format probes, unambiguous wording, N=3 unanimous-agreement runs. Tests four list types: bulleted, numbered, two-level nested, definition. Each list has a paired content-presence probe and a format-preservation probe ('is this presented AS a list, by any clear encoding'). Per Sergio's Sprint 2 finding (bullet-prefixed paragraphs are honest preservation), the format probes accept ANY clear list encoding — markdown list syntax, bullet-prefixed paragraphs, nested indentation, etc. Glossary section serves as content-presence anchor.

probes.question: Does the document mention all three of: Mercury, Venus, and Earth?
probes.ground_truth_basis: Source Section 1 contains a bulleted list of these three planets, and Section 5 Glossary lists them. Verified visually.

probes.question: In the document, are 'Mercury', 'Venus', and 'Earth' presented together as items in a bulleted (unordered) list, whether via markdown bullet syntax (- or *), bullet-character prefixes (•), nested indentation, or any other clear list-presentation signal?
probes.ground_truth_basis: Source: Section 1 \begin{itemize} with three \item entries. Rendered PDF shows three bullet-prefixed indented items. Probe accepts any clear list encoding.

probes.question: Does the document mention all three of: Red, Green, and Blue?
probes.ground_truth_basis: Source Section 2 numbered list and Section 5 Glossary.

probes.question: In the document, are 'Red', 'Green', and 'Blue' presented together as items in a numbered (ordered) list, whether via markdown ordered-list syntax (1. 2. 3.), explicit numerical prefixes, or any other clear ordered-list-presentation signal?
probes.ground_truth_basis: Source: Section 2 \begin{enumerate} with three \item entries. Rendered PDF shows '1. Red', '2. Green', '3. Blue' as indented numbered items.

probes.question: Does the document mention all of: Fruits, Apple, Banana, Vegetables, Carrot, and Daikon?
probes.ground_truth_basis: Source Section 3 nested list and Section 5 Glossary.

probes.question: In the document, are 'Apple' and 'Banana' presented as nested sub-items under the parent item 'Fruits', whether via markdown nested-list syntax (indented bullets), nested HTML lists, hierarchical indentation, or any other clear signal of nesting under a parent list item?
probes.ground_truth_basis: Source Section 3 has Fruits with a nested itemize containing Apple and Banana, and Vegetables with a nested itemize containing Carrot and Daikon. Rendered PDF shows two-level indentation with Apple/Banana sub-items below Fruits, and Carrot/Daikon sub-items below Vegetables.

probes.question: Does the document mention all three of: Compiler, Interpreter, and Linker?
probes.ground_truth_basis: Source Section 4 definition list and Section 5 Glossary.

probes.question: In the document, are 'Compiler', 'Interpreter', and 'Linker' each paired with a description (definition), whether via markdown definition-list syntax, run-in bold-term-followed-by-definition formatting, HTML dl tags, or any other clear signal that pairs each term with its description?
probes.ground_truth_basis: Source Section 4 \begin{description} with three \item[term] description entries. Rendered PDF shows each term in bold immediately followed by its definition on the same line.

probes.question: Does the document mention 'Saturn'?
probes.ground_truth_basis: Source contains Mercury, Venus, Earth as the only planets mentioned. Saturn is a thematic distractor not present in the source.

probes.question: In the document, is 'Apple' presented as a numerically ordered list item (e.g., '1. Apple') with explicit positional numbering?
probes.ground_truth_basis: Source: Apple appears as a sub-bullet under Fruits in the nested itemize (Section 3). It is not in any enumerate/numbered list. Tests whether the converter or judge falsely infers numbering where none exists.
